sentences.text: One travels so much, they don't have real time for it, so.
Yes, yes.
Very much.
Oh, from both of us?
A whole big sentence?
Depends how I'll feel after this whole ordeal is over.
[LAUGH]
So I have to talk?
Not very interesting.
We came late in the afternoon.
We came from New York, from Long Island.
And we stopped for a bite, did some marketing and came home at--
Yes, from New York, yes.
We were away for one week.
Rena Fagen.
In my birth certificate was 'Regeena--' Regina Schonthal.
S-C-H-O-N-T-H-A L.
Always as Rena. 'Renna' in Polish.
October 27, 1925.
69 years old.
In Krakow, Krakow, Poland.
My mother's name was Bella.
My father's name was Aron.
And I have an older brother.
He's five years older.
His name is Paul.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I must say, I had a very privileged childhood.
I was a daughter of well-to-do parents.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We lived in a building that was owned by my grandmothers-- by my grandmother.
And we have a-- had a comfortable apartment.
I had a happy childhood.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I attended the elementary school for six years, and with afternoon activities like dancing classes, ballet school.
And then after the sixth grade, I went to the Hebrew high school, Hebrew gymnasium.
He was an importer of fruits and nuts, anything that-- and everything that did not grow in Poland, like citrus fruit, pineapples, almonds, nuts.
No.
We had a very traditional home.
Every holiday was observed.
Friday night was a traditional evening.
Saturdays, I didn't write, I didn't write.
Well, we had a kosher home, of course.
Yes.
The name was Kipperschule.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, of course, every holiday when my parents attended, I would come there and spend a few hours during the high holidays like Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, and Passover holidays.
In Krakow, well, there were aunts and uncles and cousins.
And in our building lived my grandmother, my aunt, my uncle, and a cousin.
They lived in the same building on the first floor.
And we lived on the main floor.
I don't think I was so much aware of it.
I was still very young.
But I remember that when I attended my dancing classes, there were many gentile girls.
And I don't-- did not feel very comfortable among them.
Not that I heard any remarks or any-- or there were any anti-Semitic incidents, I just did not-- I felt much more comfortable in a Jewish environment.
Well, there were many business people.
There were quite a few professional people.
Not-- I don't remember how many.
Yes.
Yeah.
Well, we remember-- what was-- I remember about two-- was it two years before the outbreak of the World War II, and the German Jews of Polish extraction were-- were expelled from Germany.
And many of them came through [INAUDIBLE] to, let's say, to Krakow, to Poland.
And each Jewish family was hosting a person, inviting them for meals.
Some German Jews used to live in the apartments with us.
We had a gentleman who was coming every day for a main meal.
And then we-- then I heard that they are throwing out the-- the Jews out of Germany.
But it never entered our mind that it may happen to us.
No, I don't really recall it, or maybe I wasn't paying attention.
I was a young girl, 12, 13.
It just-- I was-- I was-- wasn't very political and just didn't pay attention to it.
Not exactly the-- the day by itself.
But a few days before when we-- when there was a talk of outbreak of the war, and we were taping the windows against the-- against the bombs.
There was an excitement in the air.
And I remember even telling my parents, please don't be angry at me.
But I would like to see just for two, three days to see what war looks like.
This was-- there was so much excitement.
And it was just-- war is-- was like an abstract idea.
I had no-- I didn't know what-- what it is all about.
Well, buying a little bit more food that should-- household was-- would be well-provided, things like that.
Yes.
Well, the war broke out in September.
And then a few-- few months later, we-- we were ordered to wear arm bands, white arm bands with the blue Star of David on them.
The-- in-- two or three months later, the school-- schools were closed.
But nevertheless, we managed to-- to gather in private homes.
And our professors were giving us private instructions.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Now, in-- my father and my brother, they were very fortunate.
My father had a businessman passport.
And my brother had a student visa.
And they-- the Germans let them out.
And I believe end of December of 1939, and they left for Italy, for Trieste, Italy, thinking that nothing would happen to women.
We would just stay there and take care of our possessions of the apartment and whatever was in there.
And the war will end in few months.
And they'll wait in Italy, and they'll come back.
Men were afraid that they'll be caught for the forced labor, but nothing would happen to women.
Nobody imagined that-- what's waiting, what's in store for us.
As a matter of fact, we also had papers.
And we wanted to leave, like, two months or two months later.
But the Germans wouldn't let us out.
So we stayed.
Our bank account was blocked.
We were left without any money.
And we-- we tried to-- I was the one who was trying to sell anything that was sellable in the house, like musical instruments, book, knickknacks.
And fortunately, there was a-- my mother's cousin by marriage, his name was Abram Banker, who was the owner of the Emalia enamelware factory.

And he was helping us out financially, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] saying always that if my father's survives the war, then-- if we all survive the war, then my father will certainly return all the money to him.
And my father did.
This is still 1941.
With my mother and a woman by the name of Teckla, who was a gentile Ukrainian woman.
She was my-- she was a combination of a nanny and a maid.
She came to our house when I was six months old.
And I consider her like my second mother.
She was extremely devoted.

And as a matter of fact, when-- when the Germans started raiding our Jewish quarters, the apartments, and the homes and the apartments, looking for valuables and anything that they wanted to help themselves to, this Teckla used to walk out of the apartment.
My mother had beautiful jewelry, so she sewed it into a corset.
And every time there was a search, Teckla would put it on and under different pretexts, would leave the apartment.
And this way, she-- she was able to save it for us.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, the-- the most drastic change-- well, my father left, my brother left.
This was already break up of the family.
Then the schools were closed.
And I realized we had financial problems we-- to-- to-- to support ourselves.
And thanks to this cousin Banker, we-- we managed.
We managed.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then, of course, came the-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] they were forming the ghetto [INAUDIBLE] outskirts of Krakow.
But not everybody was fortunate to be allowed to get to ghetto.
You needed a so-called [NON-ENGLISH], identity card.
And we didn't get one.
We had to leave Krakow.
As far as I can recall, you had to either work for certain-- in certain workplaces in order to-- to be entitled to-- to get it.
And anyway, we-- we couldn't get to ghetto, so we left the apartment.
We packed up whatever we could into a few valises, and we went to a small town called Wolbrom.
Our-- my mother's uncle lived there already who also was resettled from a different town.
And we felt we might as well be somewhere where we-- where we have some relatives.
We came to Wolbrom.
We lived there in one room with kitchen privileges.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we were packing to go to Wolbrom, as long as I remember, just so happened that my mother was very sick.
She had an infection in her leg.
It was high fever.
And I was the one who was in charge of packing and deciding what-- what goes, what-- what stays.
And I imagine, because I was young, I still had some hopes.
And I was optimistic about this whole-- even though I knew I am leaving my home.
But my mother noticed that I was, among other things, packing bathing suits.
So she says, Rena, where do you think you are going?
I said, well, there will be a place where we'll be able to swim.
Swimming was my-- my good sport.
Were you-- when you were packing to leave--
Yes.
I don't think-- it was a question of days.
We knew we have to get out.
But we were thinking in terms that this Teckla will stay in our apartment.
And she will take care for us for the apartment.
We-- we-- we only thought in terms, everything is temporary.
It's not-- it's not forever.
Yes, our wardrobe.
No.
Photographs stayed with Teckla.
No, we-- we did not.
She stayed for a while.
Then the apartment was assigned to a German family.
And Teckla stayed-- tried to stay there with them.
But whatever they wanted her to do, she used to say, my lady was doing things this way.
And my lady was doing things this way.
And they couldn't take it, her quoting all the time the Jewish lady of the house.
And they threw her out.
And that was the end of--
Well, she-- kept the-- she took the jewelry and little small items that she felt were of any value to-- to my mother.
So she take-- she took it, yeah.
I am not in ghetto.
I am in the small town of Wolbrom.
This was-- the Germans were not troubling themselves too much with this particular small town.
So the life was-- I-- I don't know whether to call it normal.
I mean, we were thrown out of our apartment.
But I was taking a nursing course.
I was taking a bookkeeping course.
I was taking a course in English.
I tried-- my education was interrupted.
So whatever I could, I tried to-- to learn, maybe thinking in more-- in more practical terms than academic terms.
And then after-- we were in Wolbrom about nine months.
And this-- this cousin Banker, we managed through some relatives get the needed identity cards to be admitted to ghetto.
And this cousin of ours, Banker, sent a truck.
And we came back to Krakow ghetto.
Yes.
There was a section, a Jewish-enclosed section, yes.
Yes, with gates.
Yeah.
And we were-- we were assigned into an apartment where already my-- my aunt, my grandmother, and my cousin lived.
This was a large apartment.
But it didn't really matter how many rooms there were, because each window was assigned to three people.
So if a room had three windows, there were nine people living in that room.
And the partitions were made with sheets to provide some kind of privacy.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and these-- these were the-- there was one communal kitchen where everybody had cooking privileges.
How were you supporting yourself at this time?
How are you getting money for food in the ghetto?
I-- as far as I remember, it's still this cousin Banker, who was-- who was really helping us financially.
Yeah.
Yes.
Well, in the ghetto, I-- I was working as a junior nurse in a hospital in Krakow ghetto.
I was mostly working with people who were caught in German transport [INAUDIBLE].
I would say chronically ill.
And I was allowed to do practically everything around them, except maybe giving injections.
And this was my kind of work at the beginning in ghetto.
And then I was working in Optima, which was a factory of military uniforms.
And my job was to operate either a sewing machine or a machine that was making the button holes.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Yes, we were guarded.
And I remember one-- there was an action, a resettlement in-- in June while we were working in Optima.
And we were coming out of the gates of the ghetto.
And they had many groups who worked in different places of work were going out.
And they-- and there was sort of a selection.
Certain people were allowed to go to place-- their place of work.
And certain people were motioned to go with a transport.
As we came out of the gates of the ghetto, there was Teckla standing in front of the gates with an arm band in her hands, on her wrist.
And when she saw us, that we were-- that we were sent to-- to work and not with the transport, she-- she showed us.
You see, I had that arm band.
Had I seen you going with the transport, I was ready to pull it over my shoulder and go together with you.
And this was a moment that-- we were everything to her.
And--
To me, she was always old.
But I imagine around-- between 50 and 60.
Around 60, I would say.
Yes.
We knew there were concentration camps.
But we-- we didn't know exactly what-- what is happening to those people.
Not to my knowledge.
Not that I can remember.
That's right.
Every time there was a-- we heard.
And every time was a selection or was an action, I remember the thought going through my mind.
This cannot be the end yet.
This is-- there was always a spark of hope, no matter what.
Maybe because I was so young, but always hopeful that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things would somehow work themselves out.
And, of course, it was a-- a tremendous comfort that I was all the time with my mother.
I'm trying to think.
We know-- don't know even exactly what-- when they left-- when Italy became close to introducing the anti-Semitic-- the-- the Nazi laws.
Then my-- my father and my brother went-- left for Tangier, for North Africa, Tangier in Morocco.
And we know-- we knew that much.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we were so, so busy with-- with our own life that everything else-- we knew they are safe.
And--
But there was no other forms of communication, letters--
For a while, we used to get some letters.
But then it stopped.
[DRINKS]
In ghetto, I'm trying to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] well, there were-- there was a punishment.
There were the actions, the resettlements.
The-- when people were taken with transports there was one.
In June, the one that we were going out to Optima, then there was a big one in October where many people were sent out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And trying to--
But these guards then at the time were mostly just to watch your comings and goings.
That's right.
In the Krakow ghetto-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trying to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
I'm a little bit confused with the dates.
Yes, I am working at Optima.
And then when Plaszow was formed, we-- we-- we were working-- we were-- we were moved to the camp in Plaszow.
We took our belongings, but whatever we could carry in our-- in two valises.
And we were-- we started living in Plaszow concentration camp.
No.
The-- the Krakow ghetto was on the-- the point of being liquidated.
And all the inhabitants, all the Jews were going to-- to Plaszow camp.
I really don't remember.
I don't-- I-- I couldn't.
We walked.
We walked from Krakow ghetto to-- to-- to Plaszow with whatever we could carry in our two hands.
And--
A few-- probably, yes.
As far as I can remember, yeah.
And we arrived in Plaszow.
Well, of course, this was a shock to see all the sleeping accommodations, the bunk beds.
There were three tiers of bunk beds.
If I remember correctly, my mother and I were in the middle bunk bed.
And my-- my grandmother and my aunt and my cousin had the lower-- lower bunks.
There were wooden boards with some-- well, we managed to carry with us some pillows and blankets.
So this is what-- what we used to cover ourselves.
Not when we-- not when we arrived in Plaszow.
From Plaszow, we were going out to work to Optima and coming back for the-- for the night to Plaszow.
And I remember every time we would come back, the first question was, what's the score?
How many were hung, and how many were shot?
Because there was always killing going on in Plaszow with this infamous Amon Goth was the commandant of the camp.
And there were many selections.
And I remember once we-- we were witnessing hanging of a friend of ours, a young boy who was at that time 17.
Or-- supposedly, he was humming a tune, a Russian tune.
And that was a good enough reason to-- to hang him for it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After a while, we stopped working at Optima.
And we started working for my cousins, this Abram Banker, who was the owner of the enamelware factory.
And we were coming out of-- coming out from Plaszow every day, marching five in a row.
That was my mother, I, my aunt, cousin, and my grandma.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] coming in the morning-- going out in the morning, coming in the evening.

After a while, I think it was in March [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '43-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by the way, the factory was taken over by Oskar Schindler, like every Jewish business was taken over by-- by a German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so-called [NON-ENGLISH], who was in charge of the factory.
And Oskar Schindler was assigned to take this enamelware factory.
